Article.ArticleTitle: Coral geochemical archives reveal shipbreaking-driven heavy metal pollution in the Bay of Bengal.

Abstract.AbstractText: Shipbreaking in Chittagong, Bangladesh, a major global vessel dismantling hub, is a significant source of coastal metal pollution, yet long-term records are scarce. This study provides the first high-resolution coral-based reconstruction of copper (Cu), nickel (Ni), and lead (Pb) from 2011 to 2022 at Saint Martin Island. Metal-to‑calcium ratios measured via ICP-MS were compared with a pre-industrial subfossil coral (1573 CE) to establish natural baselines, revealing 2-3.5 times higher in modern corals. Time series analysis and correlation linked these enrichments specifically to shipbreaking, while alternative sources-including river discharge, port activities, and local industries-showed no significant association. Segmented regression identified metal-specific thresholds: Cu indicated chronic baseline contamination, Ni chronic exposure, and Pb acute spikes. Seasonal cycles showed pre-monsoon peaks from intensified shipbreaking, diluted during the monsoon. Using corals as bioarchives and defining pollution thresholds, this study reveals shipbreaking's ecological footprint and provides science-based guidance for threshold-based monitoring, ship pretreatment, improved waste handling, and adoption of green recycling technologies, supporting both local regulations and international policy.
